criteria.label: State and apply the batch gradient descent update θ ← θ − η∇L(θ) on a scalar quadratic loss.
criteria.example: For L(w) = (w − 3)² at w = 0, η = 0.2: ∇L = −6, so w ← 0 − 0.2(−6) = 1.2 after one step.

criteria.label: Distinguish batch GD from mini-batch SGD by what data each gradient uses.
criteria.example: Dataset of 1,000 points: batch GD uses all 1,000 per update; batch size 32 SGD uses 32 points per update, ~32 updates per epoch.

criteria.label: Implement a full-batch training loop with correct zero_grad, backward, and step order.
criteria.example: PyTorch loop over 50 epochs: `zero_grad()` → forward on full X → `loss.backward()` → `optimizer.step()`; loss trends down on linear regression toy data.

criteria.label: Explain why −∇L is used and how η affects stability on a simple loss.
criteria.example: On L(w) = w², ∇L = 2w points uphill; η = 1.1 from w = 10 diverges by alternating sign, while η = 0.1 converges to 0.

criteria.label: Relate batch GD steps to movement on a loss surface and name when to prefer SGD instead.
criteria.example: θ traces a downhill path on L(θ); switch to mini-batch SGD when a full pass over web-scale token data is infeasible per step.
